does: This paper presents a comprehensive empirical analysis of Transformer language models across multiple scales, training and evaluating models from tens of millions to 280 billion parameters on 152 diverse tasks. The authors introduce Gopher, a 280B parameter model, and systematically measure how performance scales across different capability areas. They find that scaling provides the largest gains in reading comprehension, fact-checking, and toxic language identification, while logical and mathematical reasoning improve more slowly with scale. Beyond task performance, the paper provides detailed analysis of training data properties, model behavior patterns, and the relationship between model scale and issues like bias and toxicity, along with discussion of how large language models can be applied to AI safety problems.
connects: This work builds directly on prior scaling laws research that demonstrated performance improvements with model size, extending those findings across far more diverse tasks and larger model scales than previously studied systematically. It responds to growing questions about what capabilities emerge as language models grow larger and which domains benefit most from scale. The paper engages with emerging concerns about bias and toxicity in large models, connecting to parallel work on model fairness and safety. It also builds on Transformer architecture research and the broader lineage of neural language modeling, synthesizing findings about how architectural and dataset choices interact with scale to determine downstream performance.
enables: This systematic analysis of scaling relationships and task-specific performance profiles establishes a foundation for future model design choices and resource allocation decisions in training large language models. By documenting which capabilities scale well and which plateau, the work informs decisions about whether scale alone can address logical reasoning or if different architectures or training approaches are needed. The detailed analysis of bias and toxicity at different scales enables better-informed approaches to AI safety and harm mitigation in deployed systems. The breadth of evaluated tasks and scales provides a benchmark for comparing future models and guides research toward identifying which capabilities require scale versus those requiring architectural or methodological innovations.
